content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 34 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 119v | **Source Images:** DEU_034_page_001.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 166–167 | **Images:** DEU_034_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_034_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Then Moses went up from the plains of Moab to Mount Nebo, to the summit of Pisgah, which is opposite Jericho. And the LORD showed him all the land — Gilead as far as Dan,

**2** all of Naphtali, the land of Ephraim and Manasseh, all the land of Judah as far as the western sea,

**3** the Negeb, and the Plain — that is, the Valley of Jericho, the city of palm trees — as far as Zoar.

**4** And the LORD said to him, "This is the land of which I swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, 'I will give it to your offspring.' I have let you see it with your eyes, but you shall not cross over there."

**5** So Moses the servant of the LORD died there in the land of Moab, according to the word of the LORD.

**6** And he buried him in the valley in the land of Moab opposite Beth-peor; but no one knows the place of his burial to this day.

**7** Moses was a hundred and twenty years old when he died. His eye was undimmed, and his vigor had not fled.

**8** And the sons of Israel wept for Moses in the plains of Moab thirty days. Then the days of weeping and mourning for Moses were ended.

**9** And Joshua the son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom, for Moses had laid his hands on him. So the sons of Israel obeyed him and did as the LORD had commanded Moses.

**10** And there has not arisen a prophet since in Israel like Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face [panim el panim],

**11** none like him for all the signs and the wonders that the LORD sent him to do in the land of Egypt, to Pharaoh and to all his servants and to all his land,

**12** and for all the mighty power and all the great and awesome deeds that Moses did in the sight of all Israel.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Deuteronomy 34**

**"According to the word of the LORD" — al pi YHWH (v. 5):** The phrase *al pi YHWH* literally means "by/upon the mouth of the LORD." This is the same phrase used in Numbers 33:38 for Aaron's death and Numbers 9:18 for the cloud-directed journeys. The ancient rabbis read this as meaning Moses died "by the mouth [kiss] of the LORD" — a tradition of the most intimate possible death, without suffering or distress. The LXX renders *dia rhematos kyriou* (διὰ ῥήματος κυρίου), "through the word/utterance of the LORD," which is exact. LOGOS renders "according to the word of the LORD" to preserve the idiom while allowing the fuller range of meaning.

**"And he buried him" (v. 6):** The subject of *vayyiqbor oto* is not specified — grammatically it is third-person singular masculine. Most interpreters take the LORD himself as the subject: God buried Moses. The LXX uses *kai ethapsan auton* (καὶ ἔθαψαν αὐτόν), third-person plural — "and they buried him" — which either implies an indefinite passive or involves angels (as later tradition suggests). The MT third-person singular more naturally refers to the LORD as the one who buried Moses. LOGOS renders "he buried him," allowing the ambiguity while the context (only God knew the place) implies divine agency.

**"No one knows the place of his burial to this day" (v. 6):** The concealment of Moses' tomb is theologically and narratively significant: it prevents the site from becoming a place of veneration or pilgrimage. The phrase *ad ha-yom ha-zeh* ("to this day") is the standard formula for etiological explanations and implies that the text was composed at some distance from the events described. The LXX has *kai ouden oiden ten taphon autou heos tes hemeras tautes* (καὶ οὐδεὶς οἶδεν τὴν ταφὴν αὐτοῦ ἕως τῆς ἡμέρας ταύτης), exactly matching the MT.

**"His eye was undimmed, and his vigor had not fled" (v. 7):** *Lo-kathah einav ve-lo nas lechoh* — "his eye did not grow dim, and his moisture did not flee/depart." The word *lechoh* is hapax legomenon, understood as "vitality/freshness/moisture." The LXX renders *ou damblotheskan hoi ophthalmoi autou kai ouk ephtharan ta cheiliche autou* (οὐκ ἀμβλυώπησαν οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτοῦ, καὶ οὐκ ἐφθάρησαν τὰ χελύνεα αὐτοῦ), "his eyes did not grow dim and his form/appearance did not decay." The verse contrasts strikingly with Isaac's blindness in old age (Gen 27:1) and the general expectation of physical decline. Moses' undiminished physical state at death underscores his singular status.

**"Face to face" — panim el panim (v. 10):** The claim that the LORD knew Moses *panim el panim* — "face to face" — is the ultimate theological superlative in the Hebrew Bible for a human being. The same phrase was used in Exodus 33:11 ("the LORD used to speak to Moses face to face, as a man speaks to his friend") and Numbers 12:8 ("mouth to mouth I speak with him"). The LXX renders *prosopon kata prosopon* (πρόσωπον κατὰ πρόσωπον), "face according to face," exactly. The closing epithet sets Moses apart from all subsequent prophecy, including that of the writing prophets. No future prophet — even in the time of Elijah, Isaiah, or Jeremiah — is said to have known God as Moses did.

**"Mighty power" — yad ha-chazaqah (v. 12):** The phrase *yad ha-chazaqah* ("the strong/mighty hand") returns in the closing verse, creating an inclusio with Deuteronomy 4:34 and 6:21. The book of Deuteronomy has consistently described the Exodus deliverance in terms of God's "mighty hand and outstretched arm." The closing reference to "all the mighty power" locates Moses' greatness in what God did *through* him, not in Moses' independent capacities. The LXX renders *en pase cheiri te krataia* (ἐν πάσῃ χειρὶ τῇ κραταιᾷ), "in all the strong/mighty hand," exactly.

**Structural significance — the end of the Torah:** Chapter 34 is the conclusion not only of Deuteronomy but of the entire Pentateuch (Torah). The death of Moses leaves the narrative structurally incomplete: the land promised to Abraham has been seen but not entered. This open ending is theologically intentional in the canonical shape of the Torah — the promise of land remains before the reader, the covenant unfulfilled, pointing forward. The phrase "no prophet has arisen in Israel like Moses" (v. 10) functions as a canon-closing epitaph and simultaneously as a forward-pointing messianic marker: the expectation of one who will be like Moses (cf. Deut 18:15–18) remains open.

**LXX note on Vaticanus placement:** In Codex Vaticanus, the end of Deuteronomy (CAP ΛΘ — Chapter 39 in the LXX's chapter numbering) is visible in the right-hand column of pa_0171, followed immediately by the opening of Joshua (CAP A). The text flows without major section break, suggesting the LXX tradition treated the Historical Books as continuous with the Torah. The Vaticanus chapter numbering differs from modern versification at several points in this final chapter but the content is equivalent.

— LOGOS
